message.content: Adversaries may compromise third-party servers to stage, launch, and execute operations, leveraging these servers for Command and Control (C2) and other post-compromise activities. Instead of purchasing or renting servers, attackers exploit existing legitimate servers, including web servers, to support their operations. This tactic is employed by various threat groups such as APT16, Dragonfly, Earth Lusca, Indrik Spider, Lazarus Group, and Turla. These groups have used compromised servers for hosting C2 infrastructure, distributing malware, staging second-stage payloads, and serving fake updates. The impact of such compromises includes unauthorized access to sensitive data, disruption of services, and the spread of malware. Mitigation strategies include regular patching and updating of server software, implementing robust access controls, monitoring for unusual server activity, and employing network segmentation to limit the potential damage from compromised servers.
